title: How would you design and implement a custom collection class that integrates with the Java Collections Framework?

answers.junior: Designing a custom collection class that properly integrates with the Java Collections Framework requires implementing the appropriate abstract base class or interface from java.util. The most practical approach is to extend AbstractList, AbstractSet, or AbstractCollection, which provide skeletal implementations and only require you to implement the essential methods.
answers.middle: Designing a custom collection class that properly integrates with the Java Collections Framework requires implementing the appropriate abstract base class or interface from java.util. The most practical approach is to extend AbstractList, AbstractSet, or AbstractCollection, which provide skeletal implementations and only require you to implement the essential methods. For example, to create a read-only list you extend AbstractList and implement get(int index) and size(), while a mutable list also requires set(int index, E element), add(int index, E element), and remove(int index). The contract you must honor is the equals/hashCode contract defined by the List, Set, or Map interface you target; for instance, two List implementations must return true from equals() if they contain the same elements in the same order, regardless of implementation. You should override iterator() to return a proper fail-fast iterator that throws ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified during iteration, typically by tracking a modCount field and comparing it in the iterator's checkForComodification() method.
answers.senior: Designing a custom collection class that properly integrates with the Java Collections Framework requires implementing the appropriate abstract base class or interface from java.util. The most practical approach is to extend AbstractList, AbstractSet, or AbstractCollection, which provide skeletal implementations and only require you to implement the essential methods. For example, to create a read-only list you extend AbstractList and implement get(int index) and size(), while a mutable list also requires set(int index, E element), add(int index, E element), and remove(int index). The contract you must honor is the equals/hashCode contract defined by the List, Set, or Map interface you target; for instance, two List implementations must return true from equals() if they contain the same elements in the same order, regardless of implementation. You should override iterator() to return a proper fail-fast iterator that throws ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified during iteration, typically by tracking a modCount field and comparing it in the iterator's checkForComodification() method. For example: private class Itr implements Iterator<E> { int cursor; int expectedModCount = modCount; public void remove() { if (expectedModCount != modCount) throw new ConcurrentModificationException(); }}. Beyond the structural contract, production-quality custom collections must implement Serializable and optionally Cloneable, define a serialVersionUID, and document null-handling behavior since the Collections Framework has inconsistent null support across implementations. If you need thread safety, either synchronize externally or use the java.util.concurrent package's building blocks like ReentrantReadWriteLock rather than hand-rolling synchronization. Implementing Iterable correctly also means your collection participates in enhanced for-loops and stream pipelines via the default stream() method on Collection.
answers.tricky: Designing a custom collection class that properly integrates with the Java Collections Framework requires implementing the appropriate abstract base class or interface from java.util. The most practical approach is to extend AbstractList, AbstractSet, or AbstractCollection, which provide skeletal implementations and only require you to implement the essential methods. For example, to create a read-only list you extend AbstractList and implement get(int index) and size(), while a mutable list also requires set(int index, E element), add(int index, E element), and remove(int index). The contract you must honor is the equals/hashCode contract defined by the List, Set, or Map interface you target; for instance, two List implementations must return true from equals() if they contain the same elements in the same order, regardless of implementation. You should override iterator() to return a proper fail-fast iterator that throws ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified during iteration, typically by tracking a modCount field and comparing it in the iterator's checkForComodification() method. For example: private class Itr implements Iterator<E> { int cursor; int expectedModCount = modCount; public void remove() { if (expectedModCount != modCount) throw new ConcurrentModificationException(); }}. Beyond the structural contract, production-quality custom collections must implement Serializable and optionally Cloneable, define a serialVersionUID, and document null-handling behavior since the Collections Framework has inconsistent null support across implementations. If you need thread safety, either synchronize externally or use the java.util.concurrent package's building blocks like ReentrantReadWriteLock rather than hand-rolling synchronization. Implementing Iterable correctly also means your collection participates in enhanced for-loops and stream pipelines via the default stream() method on Collection. Consider also whether your collection should support spliterators by overriding spliterator() and providing characteristics flags (ORDERED, SIZED, DISTINCT) for optimal parallel stream performance. A practical example is a fixed-size circular buffer: extend AbstractList<E>, back it with an Object[] array, maintain head and size fields, implement get() with index arithmetic (array[(head + index) % capacity]), and increment modCount in every structural mutation to honor the fail-fast iterator contract.
